title: Three Sisters Stew

overview.description: A hearty stew of squash, beans and corn, inspired by the traditional Three Sisters combination of crops.

ingredients: 1 small butternut squash, peeled and cut into 3cm cubes
2 x 400g tins black beans, drained and rinsed
method: Heat the olive oil in a large saucepan or casserole over medium heat.
Add the onion, celery and carrots.
Add the cumin, smoked paprika, oregano and ground coriander.
Stir and cook for 30 seconds until fragrant.
Add the chopped tomatoes and vegetable stock.
Add the butternut squash and bay leaf.
Season with salt and black pepper.
Cover and cook for 20–25 minutes until the squash is beginning to soften.
Add the black beans and sweetcorn.
Continue cooking uncovered for 10–15 minutes until the squash is tender and the stew has thickened.
Mash a few pieces of squash and beans into the stew to give it extra body.

learning.did-you-know: The Three Sisters are corn, beans and squash. Traditionally, corn provides a climbing structure for beans, beans help replenish nitrogen in the soil, and squash spreads along the ground, helping retain moisture and suppress weeds.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How a small number of staple ingredients can create a complete and satisfying stew, and how squash can naturally thicken a dish as it cooks.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A nutritious, inexpensive and freezer-friendly Pantry Monday dish built around the traditional combination of corn, beans and squash.
chapman-tips: Cut the squash into evenly sized pieces so it cooks at the same rate.
Don't add the beans too early or they can become overly soft.
Mashing some of the squash into the stew creates a naturally thick sauce.
This should be a hearty stew rather than a thin soup.
The lime juice at the end keeps the dish from becoming too heavy.
The seasoning is deliberately different from the Vegetable & Black Bean Chilli, despite the overlap in ingredients.
A little smoked paprika adds depth, but the dish should still allow the squash and corn to be tasted.
You can use frozen sweetcorn and frozen squash if convenient.
notes.batch-cooking: Freezes well. Cool completely before freezing in portions.
notes.leftovers: Reheat gently with a splash of water. Excellent over jacket potatoes, rice or toast.
